ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic curry puff entry (FOODON_03301709); no specific salted egg quail egg variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific curry puff or salted egg quail egg pastry code. A0CKQ (filled pastry snack, savoury) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for curry puff or this specific product variant.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard curry puff piece as sold by Old Chang Kee, approximately 65 g, representing a typical single-serve snack portion.

nutrient_source.title: Curry Puff (Karipap) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central composite estimate for filled fried pastry with egg and potato filling
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo provides nutrient data for standard Singaporean curry puff (karipap). Values adapted to reflect the salted egg and quail egg variant: sodium is elevated due to salted duck egg yolk content; cholesterol is elevated due to quail egg and salted egg yolk; fat is slightly higher due to rich egg yolk. Core macros anchored to HPB curry puff baseline with adjustment for salted egg component referencing USDA FoodData Central entry for salted duck egg (NDB 01136 analogue). This is an adapted estimate; exact product-level lab data not publicly available.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Savoury Pastry and Filled Snacks — analogous estimate based on short-crust pastry and potato-filled savoury snack data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salted egg quail egg curry puff. GI of 55 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for savoury short-crust pastry snacks and potato-filled pastries, which cluster in the GI range 50–60. The wheat flour pastry shell and potato filling are the primary glycemic drivers. Salted egg yolk and quail egg contribute negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 17.9) / 100 ≈ 9.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from salted duck egg yolk
hypertension.guidance: Each curry puff provides approximately 338 mg sodium (65 g serving), representing 14–17% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit consumption to one piece and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour pastry with moderate glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 55 and a glycemic load of ~9.8 per serving, this curry puff has a moderate glycemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one piece, pair with a low-GI food or protein source, and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat and cholesterol from salted egg yolk and deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The salted duck egg yolk and deep-fried pastry contribute saturated fat (~3.5 g per serving) and cholesterol (~120 mg per serving); individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this snack occasionally and within the context of a diet low in saturated fat and sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salted Egg Quail Egg Curry Puff (also known as 'Onstik' in Old Chang Kee branding) is a Singaporean deep-fried savoury pastry snack sold by the iconic local chain Old Chang Kee. It features a flaky, golden short-crust wheat flour pastry shell encasing a filling of whole quail egg, salted duck egg yolk, curried potato, and aromatic spices including curry powder, onion, and chilli. The salted egg yolk imparts a rich, creamy, and intensely savoury umami flavour that contrasts with the mild curry-spiced potato filling and the delicate quail egg. The pastry is crisp and slightly oily on the outside, yielding to a moist, flavourful interior. This snack is a popular on-the-go street food and hawker item in Singapore, typically consumed as a breakfast snack, afternoon tea item, or light meal accompaniment. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy, moderate-fat snack with elevated sodium due to the salted egg component. It provides protein from the quail and salted eggs, carbohydrates from the pastry and potato, and notable cholesterol from the egg yolks. Clinically, the high sodium content warrants caution for individuals with hypertension, while the moderate GI and refined carbohydrate content require portion awareness for those managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk.
common_user_queries: What is a salted egg curry puff?
How many calories in Old Chang Kee salted egg quail egg curry puff?

confidence_reason: No exact product-level laboratory nutrient data is publicly available for Old Chang Kee Salted Egg Quail Egg Curry Puff. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo curry puff baseline with adjustments for salted egg yolk and quail egg components using USDA FoodData Central reference data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar savoury pastry snacks. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB or USDA reference sources used for this adapted estimate. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the primary reference sources. All other -1 values reflect nutrients not reported in the adapted source composite. The food_group has been corrected from the input value 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Snacks' as this is a fried pastry snack product; the egg and dairy components are ingredients within the snack rather than the primary food group classification. The food_name has been standardised from 'Salted Egg Quail Egg Onstik' to 'Salted Egg Quail Egg Curry Puff' as 'Onstik' is a proprietary Old Chang Kee brand term for their curry puff product line; the canonical English food name is curry puff. 'Onstik' is preserved as an alias. Halal status is listed as true based on Old Chang Kee's publicly stated halal certification from MUIS Singapore.

Added all mandatory Singaporean multilingual names: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
